equipment_categories.name: All Equipment
equipment_categories.description: Browse the full range of lifting and hoisting equipment available for short- and long-term hire.

equipment_categories.name: Electric Chain Hoists
equipment_categories.description: Powered chain hoists for efficient lifting on industrial and construction sites, including indoor and outdoor-rated models.

equipment_categories.name: Manual Chain Hoists
equipment_categories.description: Hand chain blocks for precise lifting and positioning where power is not available or not preferred.

equipment_categories.name: Lever Hoists
equipment_categories.description: Ratcheting lever hoists for tensioning, pulling, and lifting in tight or difficult-to-access areas.

equipment_categories.name: Beam Trolleys
equipment_categories.description: Push and geared trolleys to suspend and move hoists safely along I-beams and runway systems.

equipment_categories.name: Scaffold Hoists
equipment_categories.description: Scaffold-mounted hoists for vertical material handling on construction scaffolds and building facades.

resource_articles.title: Electric Hoist Safety Checklist for Site Supervisors
resource_articles.summary: A practical electric hoist safety checklist covering pre-use inspection, safe operation and shutdown procedures.
resource_articles.content: This safety guide provides a step-by-step electric hoist safety checklist designed for site supervisors and operators. It covers verification of rated capacity, inspection of chains and hooks, testing of limit switches, checking emergency stop functionality, and verifying power supply and IP rating suitability for the environment. The guide also outlines safe lifting practices, including correct use of slings, avoiding side-loading, exclusion zones under suspended loads, and clear communication via hand signals or radios. A printable checklist is included to support daily pre-start checks and toolbox talks.

resource_articles.title: Electric Hoist Safety Fundamentals: IP Ratings and Enclosures
resource_articles.summary: Understand IP ratings, weather protection and enclosure requirements when using electric hoists outdoors.
resource_articles.content: Selecting an electric hoist for outdoor use requires careful consideration of IP ratings and enclosure design. This guide explains the difference between IP54, IP55, IP65 and higher ratings, and how dust and water ingress protection affects long-term reliability and safety. It highlights typical hazards such as moisture in pendant controls, corrosion on terminals and condensation within motors. The guide provides recommendations for positioning control boxes, routing cables, and performing regular visual checks on seals and gaskets. It also covers the importance of RCD protection and lockable isolators when installing hoists in exposed locations.

resource_articles.title: Manual Chain Hoist Safety Basics for New Operators
resource_articles.summary: An introductory guide to safe use of manual chain blocks, including pre-use checks and operating technique.
resource_articles.content: Manual chain hoists, or chain blocks, are simple but powerful lifting devices. This guide covers the fundamentals of safe operation: confirming the rated capacity against the load, checking the identification plate, and ensuring the top suspension point is properly rated and secure. It details pre-use inspection steps for hand chain, load chain and hooks, explains how to avoid side loading and shock loading, and provides guidance on correct body positioning to reduce strain injuries. The guide also clarifies when to remove a hoist from service and how to report defects under local lifting regulations.

resource_articles.title: Lever Hoist Inspection and Maintenance Guide
resource_articles.summary: Periodic inspection and maintenance procedures for lever hoists used in lifting and pulling applications.
resource_articles.content: Lever hoists are frequently used for pulling, tensioning and lifting in demanding environments. This guide describes daily, weekly and periodic inspection routines to keep lever hoists safe and reliable. Topics include checking hooks for throat opening and twist, inspecting chains for wear and elongation, verifying freewheel operation, and ensuring the brake engages correctly under load. The document also discusses lubrication intervals, storage practices to prevent corrosion, and record-keeping requirements for statutory inspections. Clear pass/fail criteria and example defect photos help users decide when to quarantine equipment.

resource_articles.title: Beam Trolley Selection and Compatibility with Hoists
resource_articles.summary: How to select the right beam trolley and ensure safe compatibility with manual and electric hoists.
resource_articles.content: Incorrect pairing of beam trolleys and hoists can lead to unsafe loading of beams and trolley frames. This guide explains key selection criteria such as rated capacity, flange width range, minimum curve radius and compatibility with specific hoist capacities. It outlines how to measure beams, set side-plate spacing and torque adjustment bolts, and verify that anti-drop plates and wheel stops are fitted. Example compatibility tables illustrate typical pairings, including 1-ton manual hoists with 2-ton trolleys to achieve additional safety factors when traversing loads.

resource_articles.title: Scaffold Hoist Setup and Fall Protection Essentials
resource_articles.summary: Step-by-step guidance for setting up scaffold hoists and managing falling object risks.
resource_articles.content: Scaffold hoists introduce dynamic loads and falling object risks on building facades. This guide covers correct attachment of hoist brackets to standards and ledgers, verification of scaffold design suitability, and use of counterweights and tie-ins where required. It explains how to establish exclusion zones, fit debris nets or fans, and manage lifting routes to avoid overloading specific bays. The guide also highlights the need for competent persons to supervise installation and periodic checks, and outlines common non-compliances found during site inspections.

resource_articles.title: Understanding Hoist Duty Cycles and Motor Ratings
resource_articles.summary: An explanation of hoist duty classifications, motor ratings and how they affect hire applications.
resource_articles.content: Selecting an electric chain hoist for intensive lifting requires more than just capacity and height of lift. This technical note explains FEM and ISO duty classifications, typical motor duty ratings such as S2, S3 and S4, and how they translate into allowable starts per hour and run times. It provides worked examples comparing workshop maintenance lifting with high-throughput production lines, and offers guidance on when to increase hoist capacity or duty class to prevent overheating, nuisance tripping and premature brake wear.

resource_articles.title: Case Study: Rooftop Installation of an Outdoor-Rated Electric Hoist
resource_articles.summary: A project case study describing the safe installation of an IP65-rated electric hoist on a rooftop plant deck.
resource_articles.content: This case study follows a mechanical contractor tasked with installing a rooftop electric hoist to lift HVAC components. It describes how the team selected an IP65 outdoor-rated hoist, assessed existing steelwork for beam trolley installation, and implemented temporary edge protection and exclusion zones. The narrative covers coordination with crane lifts, electrical isolation procedures, and commissioning checks before handing the system over to the client. Lessons learned highlight the importance of weatherproof pendants, routing cabling away from trip hazards, and agreeing clear communication protocols between roof and ground teams.

resource_articles.title: Pre-Use Inspection Log Template for Hired Hoists
resource_articles.summary: Downloadable guidance and example templates for recording daily inspections of hired lifting equipment.
resource_articles.content: Regulations in many jurisdictions require users of hired lifting equipment to perform and document pre-use inspections. This article explains what needs to be checked before each shift for electric, manual and lever hoists. It includes example wording for checklists, guidance on defect categorisation, and an inspection log template that can be adapted to site-specific requirements. The article also clarifies how hire companies and site operators share responsibilities for thorough examinations and ongoing condition monitoring.

resource_articles.title: Safe Use of Trolleys with 1-Ton Manual Chain Hoists
resource_articles.summary: Guidance on pairing 1-ton manual chain hoists with suitable beam trolleys and runway beams.
resource_articles.content: When using 1-ton manual chain hoists with push or geared trolleys, it is critical to ensure that the beam, trolley and hoist work together as a system. This guide explains how to read load charts, check beam markings, and verify that the trolley capacity meets or exceeds the hoist rating. It also describes common operational errors such as side pulling, dragging loads and uneven loading of the beam, and provides practical tips for manual traversing on long beams and around obstructions.

resource_articles.title: Planning a Multi-Day Hoist Hire: Capacity, Height and Site Access
resource_articles.summary: Key questions to answer when specifying hoists for multi-day or multi-week hire projects.
resource_articles.content: Choosing the right hoist for a multi-day hire involves more than selecting capacity. This planning guide walks through typical information required by hire coordinators: maximum load, lifting frequency, available power, required height of lift, beam or scaffold details, and site access constraints such as delivery times and craneage. It also suggests how to plan for contingency equipment, spare pendants or chain containers, and how to minimise downtime by scheduling periodic checks during long hire periods.

resource_articles.title: Top 10 Manual Handling Tips When Using Lever Hoists
resource_articles.summary: Practical manual handling advice to reduce strain and injury when operating lever hoists.
resource_articles.content: Operating lever hoists can expose workers to awkward postures and repetitive movements. This article lists ten practical tips to reduce the risk of musculoskeletal injuries, including positioning the hoist at a comfortable height, using tag lines to control loads, working within the operator’s natural range of motion, and sharing tasks for long pulls. Illustrations show better and worse body positions, and simple warm-up routines are suggested for high-frequency operations.

resource_articles.title: Using Scaffold Hoists for Weekend Works: Permit and Supervision Requirements
resource_articles.summary: Lessons learned from weekend scaffold hoist operations on a city-centre refurbishment project.
resource_articles.content: Weekend work with scaffold hoists often involves reduced staffing and different supervision arrangements. This case-based guidance describes how a contractor planned and controlled Saturday–Monday lifting operations to minimise disruption to neighbouring businesses. It covers permit-to-work procedures, designation of a lifting supervisor, communication with building management and local authorities, and arrangements for securing hoists and materials outside working hours.

resource_articles.title: Electric Hoist Safety Around Live Production Lines
resource_articles.summary: Control measures for using electric chain hoists near conveyors, packaging lines and other moving machinery.
resource_articles.content: Working with electric hoists near live production equipment introduces additional entanglement, trapping and collision hazards. This guide discusses coordination with production teams, lockout-tagout expectations, and the use of physical barriers and interlocks. It explains how to plan lifting routes that avoid overflying active conveyors, and how to position pendant controls to keep operators outside danger zones. Example risk assessments illustrate typical control measures used in food and beverage plants.

resource_articles.title: Quick Reference: Hoist Signal Person Hand Signals
resource_articles.summary: Illustrated quick reference for standard hand signals used during hoisting operations.
resource_articles.content: Clear communication between hoist operators and signal persons is essential, especially on noisy construction sites. This quick reference manual summarises commonly used hand signals for lifting, lowering, emergency stop, inching, and directional travel when using trolleys. It explains who may act as a signal person, how to establish line-of-sight and backup communication methods, and suggests briefings to ensure all team members understand the agreed signal set before lifting begins.
